coredata.dc:title: Trypsin stabilized copper nanoclusters as a highly sensitive and selective probe for fluorescence sensing of morin and temperature
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xian-song
Zhang, Shen
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a platform for sensitive fluorescence sensing of morin was developed based on the copper nanoclusters, which was carried out by using a one-pot method. The copper nanoclusters (Trypsin@Cu NCs) were fabricated through CuCl2 as a metal precursor, trypsin as a protective agent and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent. The Trypsin@Cu NCs exhibited blue fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 378 nm. Using Trypsin@Cu NCs as the patterned nanosensors, the fluorescent “turn off” phenomenon was developed for the measurement of morin with the linear decline of emissive intensity at 459 nm. The fluorescence quenching reason was subjected to the static quenching and inner filter effect. Under the optimal conditions, an excellent linear response between ln(F0/F) of Trypsin@Cu NCs and morin amounts in the light of the range of 0.5–150 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.083 μM. Moreover, this proposed nanosensor could be applied for the sensing of temperature. This sensing method was displayed to be a practical determination system for the measurement of morin in real samples, which could open new horizons to develop novel detection models for morin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Trypsin
Inner filter effect
Morin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing sensitivity of Trypsin@Cu NCs towards morin
Sensing of morin in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the Scientific Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences ( R2019SHH06 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of Trypsin@Cu NCs; (B) The XPS spectrum of Trypsin@Cu NCs; (C) The XPS spectrum of copper;(D) The FT-IR spectra of Trypsin@Cu NCs and trypsin.
(A) The excitation and emission spectra of Trypsin@Cu NCs; (B) The emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of Trypsin@Cu NCs with different concentration of morin; (B) The linear relationship graph between ln(F0/F) and the concentration of morin (0.5–60 μM); (C) The linear relationship graph between ln(F0/F) and the concentration of morin (60–150 μM).

A) The plot of Trypsin@Cu NCs with different sensing substances; (B) The fluorescence color of the Trypsin@Cu NCs solution with different substances (from left to right: blank, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Br-, I-, CO3 2-, threonine, glycine, uric acid, alanine, histidine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, isoleucine, methionine, leucine, proline, serine, penicillin, kanamycin, ascorbic acid, trypsin and morin).

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of morin, Trypsin@Cu NCs and Trypsin@Cu NCs + Morin; (B) The linear relationship graph between F0/F and the concentration of morin (0.5–20); (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of morin, the excitation and emission spectra of Trypsin@Cu NCs; (D) The fluorescence lifetime of Trypsin@Cu NCs with and without morin.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of Trypsin@Cu NCs at different temperature; (B) The linear relationship graph between fluorescence intensities and temperature (25–60 °C); (C) The cycle index of Trypsin@Cu NCs between 15 °C and 65 °C.
Schematic illustration of determination strategy for morin based on Trypsin@Cu NCs.
Comparison of different analytical methods for morin sensing.
The morin detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Trypsin stabilized copper nanoclusters as a highly sensitive and selective probe for fluorescence sensing of morin and temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Environmental Materials & Remediation Technologies, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences, Yongchuan 402160, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Environmental Materials & Remediation Technologies, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Environmental Materials & Remediation Technologies, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences, Yongchuan 402160, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a platform for sensitive fluorescence sensing of morin was developed based on the copper nanoclusters, which was carried out by using a one-pot method. The copper nanoclusters (Trypsin@Cu NCs) were fabricated through CuCl2 as a metal precursor, trypsin as a protective agent and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent. The Trypsin@Cu NCs exhibited blue fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 378 nm. Using Trypsin@Cu NCs as the patterned nanosensors, the fluorescent “turn off” phenomenon was developed for the measurement of morin with the linear decline of emissive intensity at 459 nm. The fluorescence quenching reason was subjected to the static quenching and inner filter effect. Under the optimal conditions, an excellent linear response between ln(F0/F) of Trypsin@Cu NCs and morin amounts in the light of the range of 0.5–150 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.083 μM. Moreover, this proposed nanosensor could be applied for the sensing of temperature. This sensing method was displayed to be a practical determination system for the measurement of morin in real samples, which could open new horizons to develop novel detection models for morin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Morin (Fig. S1), as one of the flavonoids, mainly exists in the roots and barks of South American yellowwood trees and Chinese mulberry trees. Morin is mainly extracted from the roots or woody parts of yellow wood trees after soaking in ethanol [1,2]. Morin has good anti-oxidant and anti-cancer effects, anti-oxidation and lowering blood sugar and other functions, and has been widely used for the treatment of viral infections, stomach problems, chronic inflammation and coronary heart disease [3–5]. Moreover, morin possesses the ability to effectively scavenge free radicals, so it has received more and more attention and is used in cosmetics industry and beauty health food [6]. As it relates to people's daily life, so it is very necessary to accurately detect the concentration of morin in drinking water systems and pharmaceutical samples. Therefore, it is very necessary to develop some fast, facile and highly selective and sensitive strategies for the trace determination of morin. Nowadays, the main strategies for determination of morin are electrochemical methods [7–9], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], high performance liquid chromatography with ultra-violet detection (HPLC-UV) [12], high performance liquid chromatography with a diode array detector (HPLC-DAD) [13], UV-Vis spectrometry [14], nuclear magnetic spectroscopy (NMR) [15], fluorescence methods [16–21]. Compared with fluorescence methods, some shortcomings of these traditional methods, such as complex processes, time-consuming, expensive equipment and professional operator, have been observed, which limited their practical detection applications for the analysis of substances. Conversely, series of advantages of fluorescence methods have been obtained during the actual inspection, such as simple operation, convenient processes and less time-consuming. To date, the sensing of morin based on different fluorescence probes have been widely studied by some researchers. For example, Yan et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on the B, N dual-doped carbon dots for morin sensing [16]. Meng et al. prepared an orange emission carbon dots for morin assay based on the static quenching with the linear range of 5–125 μM and LOD of 0.84 μM [17]. Liu et al. developed a fluorescent method through “fluorescence turn-off” of water-soluble carbon dots by morin and this proposed CDs were applied for morin detecting [19]. Furthermore, Xiao et al. fabricated a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-readout nanoprobe based on Cl, N-CQDs for the detection of morin with the linear range of 0.44–54.07 μM [20]. Among these morin probes, the preparation of CDs or doped-CDs demanded more severe conditions, such as higher temperature, nitrogen protection, higher pressure, longer cultivation time. Compared with CDs, metal nanoclusters not only have the similar optical properties of CDs, but also its preparation conditions are relatively mild, such as low temperature, atmospheric pressure and moderate pH value.

In recent years, metal nanoclusters, as a new type of nanomaterial, have fascinated widespread interest due to their gainful properties, such as simple preparation, adjustable emission wavelength, good dispersion, outstanding biocompatibility, smaller size and low toxicity [22–30]. Metal nanoclusters mainly included gold nanoclusters (Au NCs), silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs), copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs), platinum nanoclusters (Pt NCs), etc [31,32]. Especially, due to the low price of copper, Cu NCs have received huge research attention in many application areas [33]. Generally, series of reducing agents were applied for the development of Cu NCs, such as hydrazine hydrate, ascorbic acid, sodium borohydride, hydroxylamine hydrochloride, formaldehyde, glucose, etc [34–36]. In short, the Cu2+ ions were reduced to Cu+ or Cu0, which could complex with organic compounds to form Cu NCs [37,38]. The most widespread protectants for establishment of Cu NCs were biomacromolecules and small molecules, such as nucleic acids, proteins, peptides, macromolecules, amino acids, thiols, etc [39–41]. For instance, a tryptophan-protected copper nanoclusters (Trp-Cu NCs) probe was developed by using a one-pot reaction for pH sensing with the linear range of 3.0–9.0 [42]. Chauhan et al. established red-emitting bovine serum albumin-fabricated copper nanoclusters (BSA-Cu NCs) for the measurement of vitamin B6 cofactors and nitroaromatics [43]. Anand et al. successfully prepared glutathione capped copper nanoclusters according to fluorescence changes for the determination of biliverdin and bilirubin [44]. Lin et al. successfully developed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe (Cu NCs) for the sensing of rutin and picric acid, and the LOD were 0.84 μM and 0.27 μM, respectively [45]. Zhang et al. fabricated tannic acid-protected copper nanoclusters, which were firstly employed to determine luteolin [46]. Moreover, Demirhan et al. successfully developed L-cysteine capped-copper nanoclusters by one-step aqueous method for the measurement of Ponceau 4R [47]. Among these stabilizers, the biomacromolecules have attracted a lot of attention because of the large number of reactive functional groups, which ensured that Cu NCs possessed good water solubility and stability for further use.

Here in this paper, blue-emitting copper nanoclusters were developed though trypsin as a protective agent and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent. The as-prepared Cu NCs were established as a fluorescent sensor for the micro-detection of morin. As exhibited in Scheme. 1 the CuCl2 solution was introduced into the trypsin solution with stirring for 5 min, then the hydrazine hydrate was added into the above mixture at the proper temperature. The as-developed Trypsin@Cu NCs possessed excellent water solubility and strong blue fluorescence. The Trypsin@Cu NCs were applied for the determination of morin based on the fluorescence quenching properties. Under optimal assay conditions, the sensing process was accomplished within 40 s, and the linear range of 0.5–150 μM and detection limit of 0.083 μM were obtained. Furthermore, due to the fluorescence weakening phenomenon under high temperature, the Trypsin@Cu NCs could be applied as a temperature sensor. Additionally, this developed preparation strategy was facile, fast and inexpensive. Finally, the Trypsin@Cu NCs-based fluorescence probe was successfully employed to determine morin in real samples with satisfactory recovery rates and relative standard deviation.

The F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and 2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) were employed to study the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption data of Trypsin@Cu NCs, respectively. For the measurement of FTIR, potassium bromide powders were completely dried and employed as adhesive for sample preparation. The FTIR data of Trypsin@Cu NCs and trypsin were performed on a Nicolet Magna 550 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Scientific, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS, United States, ESCALAB 250XI) was used to investigated the elemental composition and valence of Trypsin@Cu NCs. The TEM images of Trypsin@Cu NCs were obtained on FEI Tecnai F30 (United States) with the voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence color of Trypsin@Cu NCs were performed on the UV obscura light (ZF-1). The fluorescence pictures of Trypsin@Cu NCs were obtained by using Huawei P30 cell phone under UV light. The pH values of solution were recorded through the FE20 pH-meter (Mettler Toledo Instrument Inc., Shanghai, China).

All the materials were directly employed without any purification. Copper chloride (CuCl2, 99.99 %) and trypsin (biotech grade) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (China). Hydrazine hydrate (80 %) was obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. NaCl (99.5 %), KCl (99.5 %), MgCl2 (99 %), CaCl2 (≥ 99 %), FeCl3 (99 %), ZnCl2 (≥ 98 %), MnCl2 (99 %), NaBr (99 %), NaI (99.5 %), Na2CO3 (98 %), threonine (99 %), glycine (≥ 99 %), uric acid (99 %), alanine (99 %), histidine (≥ 99 %), glutamic acid (99 %), tryptophan (99 %), isoleucine (99 %), methionine (99 %), leucine (99 %), proline (99 %), serine (99 %), penicillin (98 %), kanamycin (97 %), ascorbic acid (> 99 %), morin (95 %) were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Millipore Milli-Q was used to output Ultra-pure water (18.25 MΩ cm−1).

The Trypsin@Cu NCs were fabricated by using trypsin as the templating agent and hydrazine hydrate as the reducing agent with minor modifications [38]. In short, CuCl2 solution (10 mL, 0.01 M) was introduced into the trypsin solution (10 mL, 40 mg/mL) at ambient temperature with stirring for 5 min. Then, the hydrazine hydrate solution (1 mL, 0.1 M) was dropped into the CuCl2 + trypsin mixture at 70 °C for 3 h. The color of solution changed from colorless to light yellow, exhibiting the successful preparation of Trypsin@Cu NCs. Furthermore, the Trypsin@Cu NCs were purified through dialysis bag (MW: 4500 Da). Finally, the purified Trypsin@Cu NCs were refrigerated in the refrigerator for further use.

The sensing of morin was investigated at ambient temperature. Series of amounts of morin were dropped into the Trypsin@Cu NCs solutions (1 mL Trypsin@Cu NCs + 1 mL H2O), and then the fluorescence spectrophotometer was employed to study the fluorescence data (spectra and intensities). To obtain the error bars, the all experiments were tested three times. Then, the linear range and LOD were developed through the above data. To detailed study the selectivity of this nano-detection platform for morin, some reference substances were introduced into the sensing system as a replacement for morin (the concentration was 150 μM), such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Br-, I-, CO3 2-, threonine, glycine, uric acid, alanine, histidine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, isoleucine, methionine, leucine, proline, serine, penicillin, kanamycin, ascorbic acid and trypsin. At the same time, the anti-interference behavior of this determination platform was further assessed. Briefly, series of controls were successively introduced into the Trypsin@Cu NCs solutions for the measurement of the fluorescence intensities. Hereafter, morin was added into the Trypsin@Cu NCs solutions with interfering substances, the corresponding fluorescence spectra and intensities were consecutively obtained under the same excitation wavelength.

The feasibility of the proposed nanosensor was investigated through measuring morin concentrations in bovine serum samples and Yili milk samples. The bovine serum samples were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The Yili milk samples were purchased from the school supermarket. The real samples were percolated to remove suspended particles by using filter paper, and the pretreated samples were diluted in the presence of Ultra-pure water. In practice, the fluorescent system was introduced into the fluorescence cuvette, and the spiked samples were then added into the above solutions. The corresponding fluorescence data were obtained by molecular fluorometer. Series of recovery rates were employed to evaluate the reliability of this system based on the calculation formula (1).
(1)
R ecovery rate =
Recorded
concentration
Added
concentration
× 100 %

The Quantum yield (QY) value of the Trypsin@Cu NCs was investigated based on the reported literature [48,49]. The calculation process of QY value was based on formula (2).
(2)
Φ =
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
× Φ R

Where Φ represents the quantum yield of Trypsin@Cu NCs, I refers to the integral area of fluorescent spectrum (at 459 nm), η represents the refractive index of solvent, A represents the UV–vis absorbance at the excitation wavelength of 378 nm. R is the quinine sulfate.

The morphology and size distribution of Trypsin@Cu NCs were characterized by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As exhibited in Fig. 1A, the Trypsin@Cu NCs were globular in shape and good dispersion, and no obvious aggregation phenomenon was found.

To further study the Surface element valences and functional groups of Trypsin@Cu NCs, the XPS and FTIR technologies were performed under the corresponding conditions. As depicted in Fig. 1B, five peaks around 196.81 eV, 286.09 eV, 399.45 eV, 532.38 eV, 934.66 eV were displayed in the total XPS spectrum of Trypsin@Cu NCs, which were assumed to S2p, C1s, N1s, O1s and Cu2p, respectively [50]. The XPS single spectrum of Cu2p of Trypsin@Cu NCs in Fig. 1C exhibited two distinct characteristic peaks at 932.41 eV (Cu2p3/2) and 951.96 eV (Cu2p1/2) [51]. In Fig. 1D, the stretching vibration of NH, CH, CO, COC bonds.

were exhibited at 3405.47 cm−1, 2962.64 cm−1, 1632.67 cm−1, 1124.20 cm−1, respectively. The asymmetric and symmetric deformation vibration of C-H bond were indicated at 1361.65 cm−1 and 1437.19 cm−1, respectively. The deformation vibration at 1540.07 cm−1 was assigned to NH bond [52,53]. Furthermore, the fluorescence feature of Trypsin@Cu NCs was investigated by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excitation and emission wavelengths were first investigated. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were exhibited at 378 nm and 459 nm through multiple corrections ( Fig. 2A). At the same time, the photographs of Trypsin@Cu NCs solution under solar lamps and ultraviolet lamps were inserted into Fig. 2A. The Trypsin@Cu NCs solution displayed light yellow and blue under solar lamps and ultraviolet lamps. The effects of excitation wavelength on the emission wavelength were further studied. As exhibited in Fig. 2B, the emission wavelength kept changing at different excitation wavelengths, which indicated that Trypsin@Cu NCs had excitation-dependent characteristics. And this characteristic might be related to structural properties [54,55]. The quantum yield of Trypsin@Cu NCs was 3.47 %. All characterization data exhibited that trypsin have been successfully introduced into the Cu NCs.

The stability of Trypsin@Cu NCs under NCl aqueous solution, daylight lamp and UV lamp were studied and displayed in Fig. S2. Under different amounts of NaCl (0.05–0.35 M), no apparent variation of the fluorescence intensity of the Trypsin@Cu NCs was found, which was favorable for the actual utilizations of Trypsin@Cu NCs at a range of concentrations of salt solution (Fig. S2A). Additionally, the photostability of metal nanoclusters was one of the necessary features that pinpointed the applications in bioassays, cell imaging, drug identification [56,57]. In short, the photostability of Trypsin@Cu NCs were studied under daylight lamp and UV lamp. As exhibited in Fig. S2B and Fig. S2C, the fluorescence intensities of Trypsin@Cu NCs were basically maintained at the same value after 10 days of sunlight exposure and 10 min of UV exposure (at room temperature). The brilliant fluorescence features made the Trypsin@Cu NCs possess hidden application values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing sensitivity of Trypsin@Cu NCs towards morin
ce:section.ce:para: The as-developed Trypsin@Cu NCs could be employed for sensing morin. The reaction time was first studied. As shown in Fig. S3, the difference in fluorescence intensity quickly reached maximum value after 40 s. As displayed in Fig. 3A, as the amounts of morin raised, the fluorescence intensities of the Trypsin@Cu NCs cut down gradually. The relative fluorescence values (ln(F0/F)) possessed an excellent linear relation with the amounts of morin in the scope of 0.5–150 μM (Fig. 3B and Fig. 3C), where the F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of Trypsin@Cu NCs without and with morin, respectively. The linear regression equation were ln(F0/F) = 0.018[Morin] + 0.025 and ln(F0/F) = 0.011[Morin] + 0.44 with the range of 0.5–60 μM and 60–150 μM, and the linear regression coefficients were 0.9941 and 0.9967, respectively. The detection limit of Trypsin@Cu NCs for morin was 0.083 μM based on the 3σ/k. Compared with reported measurement platform, this platform possessed a wider sensing range or lower LOD. Meanwhile, the comparing results were displayed in Table 1.

To affirm the selectivity of the as-developed Trypsin@Cu NCs nanosensor for morin sensing, the influences of series of possible Interfering substances like Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Br-, I-, CO3 2-, threonine, glycine, uric acid, alanine, histidine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, isoleucine, methionine, leucine, proline, serine, penicillin, kanamycin, ascorbic acid and trypsin on the fluorescence change of the nanosensor were explored under the same conditions. The relative fluorescence intensity varieties with different controls were exhibited in Fig. 4A. As shown in Fig. 4A, fluorescence intensities of the Trypsin@Cu NCs displayed a significant weakening through mixing morin with Trypsin@Cu NCs solutions, while different controls did not affect the fluorescence intensities of Trypsin@Cu NCs solutions. The ultimate specificity of this method was based on the interaction between the nanosensor and the analyte. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color of the Trypsin@Cu NCs solution with different substances was also exhibited in Fig. 4B. Thus, the Trypsin@Cu NCs nanosensor was expected to be used for morin sensing in real samples.

According to reported studies, the potential fluorescence quenching mechanism could be roughly divided into static quenching, dynamic quenching, energy transfer, charge transfer, inner filter effect, etc [58,59]. To study the exact quenching mechanism of Trypsin@Cu NCs for morin, the corresponding characterization tests were performed. Generally, the ligand chains of Cu NCs and analyte could generate a complex through hydrogen bond or electrostatic interaction, which could be applied to differentiate static quenching or dynamic quenching. Additionally, the formation of non-luminescent ground-state complexes between fluorescent molecules and quenchers could be employed to confirm the existence of static quenching. Thence, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Trypsin@Cu NCs, morin, and Trypsin@Cu NCs+morin solution were explored. As exhibited in Fig. 5A, a new peak at 420 nm was found in the UV–vis absorption spectra of Trypsin@Cu NCs+morin solution, suggesting the existence of static quenching. To affirm our guess, the Stern-Volmer Eq. (3) was performed in the following experiments [60].
(3) F0/F = 1 + K SV[Q] = 1 + K q τ 0[Q]

where F0 and F stands for the FL intensities of the Trypsin@Cu NCs in the absence and presence of morin, respectively. K SV refers to the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [Q] stands for the morin concentration. τ 0 is the FL lifetime of the Trypsin@Cu NCs (3.78 ns). Based on the Stern-Volmer equation and linear fit curve (Fig. 5B), the K SV value of 5.1 × 104 L mol−1 and K q value of 5.1 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1 were obtained. The K q value was significantly greater than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant (2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1), indicating that our guess was correct [61,62].

Furthermore, the relationship between the excitation or emission spectra of Trypsin@Cu NCs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of morin were explored and exhibited in Fig. 5C. Surprisingly, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of morin significantly overlapped with the excitation spectrum of Trypsin@Cu NCs, exhibiting that another quenching mechanism might be the inner filter effect or resonance energy transfer. The fluorescence lifetimes of Trypsin@Cu NCs with and without morin could be employed to distinguish the above two mechanisms [63]. Based on the Fig. 5D, the lifetimes of Trypsin@Cu NCs with and without morin were 3.42 ns and 3.78 ns, respectively. The constant value of lifetime proved that the inner filter effect was the quenching mechanism [64]. Thence, the static quenching and inner filter effect were main mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of morin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The as-established nanoprobe was employed for the morin detection in real samples including bovine serum samples and Yili milk samples. The pretreated samples were employed to prepare morin spiked samples. The morin levels in the spiked samples were investigated based on the linear fit curve. The recoveries of morin were 97.2–105.9 % ( Table 2). According to the results, the Trypsin@Cu NCs as a fluorescent probe were applicable for the sensing of morin in real samples.

Surprisingly, the fluorescence intensities of Trypsin@Cu NCs exhibited obviously temperature-dependent characteristic. As displayed in Fig. 6A, the FL intensities dropped gradually by adjusting the temperature from 15 to 65 °C. Meanwhile, a red-shift of the emission wavelength was found when the temperature rose. The plot was obtained between the FL intensities and temperature. The linear equation was F = − 20.92[temperature] + 1857 (25–60 °C) (Fig. 6B). More interestingly, the FL intensities would recover after the temperature dropping to 15 °C and the Trypsin@Cu NCs could be recycled multiple times (Fig. 6C). The above results expanded the value of nanosensor in terms of temperature probe.

In summary, a facile, green and efficient strategy for developing blue fluorescent copper nanoclusters was suggested in this paper. When the as-developed Trypsin@Cu NCs were used to detect potential substances in the human body, only morin could display an evident weakening for the fluorescence intensities of Trypsin@Cu NCs. Thus, the Trypsin@Cu NCs could be endued a fluorescent sensor for morin sensing based on the fluorescence quenching. The relative fluorescence values (ln(F0/F)) possessed an excellent linear relation with the amounts of morin in the scope of 0.5-150 μM, and The detection limit of Trypsin@Cu NCs for morin was 0.083 μM based on the 3σ/k. Additionally, it suggested that the Trypsin@Cu NCs could act as temperature detection as well. Thus, we firmly confirmed that such a nanoprobe could open new fields of application.
Xian-song Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shen Zhang: measure fluorescence, measure UV–visible spectra, grammar correction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Scientific Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences (R2019SHH06) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129458.
